title: Stripe webhook signing secret in client bundle
description: Stripe whsec_* values must live server-side. If they show up in client code, attackers can forge webhook events.
evidence: Stripe webhook secret (whsec_*) detected in {{url}} client-accessible resource.
riskImpact: An attacker with the webhook signing secret can forge any Stripe event — payments.succeeded, invoice.paid, subscription.deleted — causing your server to credit fraudulent orders, cancel real subscriptions, or trigger payouts without any actual transaction.
explanation: Stripe whsec_* values must live server-side. If they show up in client code, attackers can forge webhook events.
fixSteps: Move the webhook secret to an environment variable (STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET)
Verify webhooks server-side only using stripe.webhooks.constructEvent()
Rotate the secret in the Stripe Dashboard under Webhooks

codeExamples.label: Next.js Route Handler — server-side webhook verification
.env.local — secret storage
codeExamples.code:
# .env.local (never commit this file)
STRIPE_SECRET_KEY=sk_live_...
STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET=whsec_...

# .gitignore
.env
.env.local
.env*.local

title: Google Maps API key in source
description: Google Maps API keys (AIzaSy*) in client code can be abused to bill against your account.
evidence: Google Maps API key (AIzaSy*) detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: An unrestricted Google Maps API key found in client code lets anyone generate maps requests billed to your account, potentially resulting in thousands of dollars in unexpected charges before the key is noticed and revoked.
explanation: Google Maps API keys (AIzaSy*) in client code can be abused to bill against your account.
fixSteps: Restrict the key by HTTP referrer to your domain(s) in Google Cloud Console
Set an API quota limit to cap maximum daily spend
Rotate the key if it has been exposed without referrer restrictions

codeExamples.label: Google Cloud — restrict API key by referrer (gcloud CLI)
codeExamples.code:
# Restrict the Maps API key to your domains only
gcloud services api-keys update YOUR_KEY_ID \
  --api-target=service=maps-backend.googleapis.com \
  --browser-key-allowed-referrers='https://yourdomain.com/*,https://www.yourdomain.com/*'

codeExamples.label: Server-side proxy for Maps (recommended for high-traffic)

title: Google OAuth client_secret in source
description: Google client_secret values must never appear in client-side code.
evidence: Google OAuth client_secret detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: An exposed OAuth client_secret lets an attacker impersonate your application in the OAuth flow, exchange authorization codes for access tokens, and access Google APIs (Drive, Gmail, Calendar) on behalf of any user who has authorized your app.
explanation: Google client_secret values must never appear in client-side code.
fixSteps: Move client_secret to a server-only environment variable
Use the OAuth Authorization Code flow with PKCE for browser clients (no client_secret needed)
Rotate the secret immediately in Google Cloud Console if it has been exposed

codeExamples.label: Server-only OAuth — store secret as env var
Public client — use PKCE, no client_secret

title: Firebase API key (public) in source
description: Firebase Web API keys (AIzaSy*) are public by design. Security depends on Firestore rules / App Check.
evidence: Firebase Web API key (AIzaSy*) detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Firebase Web API keys are intentionally public — their presence alone is not a vulnerability. The risk is that weak Firestore or RTDB security rules combined with the exposed key allow unauthenticated read or write access to your entire database.
explanation: Firebase Web API keys (AIzaSy*) are public by design. Security depends on Firestore rules / App Check.
fixSteps: Enable Firebase App Check to block unauthorized clients
Review Firestore and RTDB security rules to ensure no data is readable without authentication
Restrict the API key to specific Firebase services in Google Cloud Console

codeExamples.label: Firestore rules — require authentication
Firebase App Check — enforce in production

title: AWS Secret Access Key in source
description: AWS secret access keys (40-char base64) must never be in client code or public buckets.
evidence: AWS secret access key detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: An exposed AWS Secret Access Key paired with an Access Key ID gives attackers full programmatic access to your AWS account at the permissions level of that IAM user — this can mean reading S3 buckets, spinning up EC2 instances, creating new IAM users, or incurring six-figure compute bills before you notice.
explanation: AWS secret access keys (40-char base64) must never be in client code or public buckets.
fixSteps: Rotate the key in IAM immediately (deactivate old key, create new key)
Check CloudTrail for API calls made with the compromised key
Use IAM Roles and short-lived STS credentials instead of long-lived access keys

codeExamples.label: Rotate compromised AWS credentials
codeExamples.code:
# 1. Create a new key
aws iam create-access-key --user-name YOUR_USER

# 2. Update your environment/secrets manager with the new key

# 3. Deactivate the old key
aws iam update-access-key --access-key-id AKIA... --status Inactive --user-name YOUR_USER

# 4. Check CloudTrail for unauthorized usage
aws cloudtrail lookup-events \
  --lookup-attributes AttributeKey=AccessKeyId,AttributeValue=AKIA...

codeExamples.label: Use IAM Role (recommended — no long-lived keys)

title: GitHub PAT in source
description: ghp_* / gho_* / ghu_* / ghs_* / ghr_* tokens grant repo / user access.
evidence: GitHub Personal Access Token (ghp_*/gho_*/ghu_*/ghs_*/ghr_*) detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: A leaked GitHub PAT gives an attacker access to every repository and organization the token owner can reach — including private repos, source code, CI/CD secrets, and deployment keys. Fine-grained tokens are scoped, but classic PATs are often overprivileged.
explanation: ghp_* / gho_* / ghu_* / ghs_* / ghr_* tokens grant repo / user access.
fixSteps: Revoke the token immediately via GitHub Settings → Developer Settings → Personal Access Tokens
Audit the token's usage in the GitHub security log before revoking
Switch to fine-grained tokens with the minimum required scopes and expiry dates

codeExamples.label: Revoke and audit the compromised token
codeExamples.code:
# Check recent usage for the token (replace TOKEN with the actual token)
curl -H 'Authorization: token TOKEN' https://api.github.com/user

# List recent events that may have used the token
curl -H 'Authorization: token TOKEN' https://api.github.com/users/OWNER/events

# Then revoke immediately at:
# https://github.com/settings/tokens

codeExamples.label: Use GitHub Actions secrets instead of hard-coded tokens
codeExamples.code:
# .github/workflows/deploy.yml
jobs:
  deploy:
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          # Use the automatically-provided GITHUB_TOKEN
          token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
      # Never hard-code a PAT in workflow files
      # If you need a PAT, store it in GitHub Actions Secrets:
      - run: gh release create v1.0.0
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.MY_PAT }}

title: NPM auth token in source
description: npm_ tokens allow publishing packages as you. Should never be in client code or public repos.
evidence: NPM authentication token (npm_*) detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: An exposed npm token allows an attacker to publish malicious versions of any package you maintain — a supply-chain attack that can inject backdoors into every application that installs your package, potentially affecting millions of downstream users.
explanation: npm_ tokens allow publishing packages as you. Should never be in client code or public repos.
fixSteps: Revoke the token immediately: npm token revoke TOKEN_ID (find ID with: npm token list)
Audit recent package publishes for unauthorized versions
Create a new granular automation token scoped only to the packages that need it

codeExamples.label: Revoke and rotate npm token
codeExamples.code:
# List all tokens to find the compromised one
npm token list

# Revoke the compromised token (use the ID from the list)
npm token revoke <token-id>

# Create a new granular token with minimal permissions
npm token create --read-only          # for CI that only installs
npm token create --cidr=10.0.0.0/8   # restrict to internal IPs

codeExamples.label: GitHub Actions — use NPM_TOKEN secret
codeExamples.code:
jobs:
  publish:
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'
          registry-url: 'https://registry.npmjs.org'
      - run: npm publish
        env:
          # Never hard-code the token — store it as a GitHub Actions secret
          NODE_AUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.NPM_TOKEN }}

title: PyPI token in source
description: pypi-AgEIcHlwaS... tokens allow uploading packages.
evidence: PyPI API token (pypi-*) detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: An exposed PyPI token allows an attacker to upload a malicious version of any Python package associated with the token, injecting arbitrary code into the install process for every user that runs pip install on that package.
explanation: pypi-AgEIcHlwaS... tokens allow uploading packages.
fixSteps: Revoke the token immediately via PyPI account settings → API tokens
Audit recent package uploads for your account
Create a new token scoped to specific projects (not the whole account)

codeExamples.label: GitHub Actions — use PyPI Trusted Publisher (no token needed)
codeExamples.code:
# Use OIDC-based Trusted Publishers instead of tokens
jobs:
  publish:
    permissions:
      id-token: write  # Required for OIDC
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: python -m build
      - uses: pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1
        # No password/token needed — OIDC handles auth

codeExamples.label: .pypirc — never commit this file
codeExamples.code:
# ~/.pypirc (user-level, never commit to source control)
[pypi]
username = __token__
password = pypi-AgEIcHlwaS...

# Add to .gitignore:
# .pypirc

title: Cloudflare API key in source
description: Cloudflare API tokens look like 40-char hex strings and grant DNS / WAF / Workers access.
evidence: Cloudflare API key or token detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: An exposed Cloudflare API token can be used to modify DNS records (pointing your domain to attacker infrastructure), disable WAF rules (exposing your origin), purge cache, or exfiltrate Workers secrets — impacting availability and security for all users of your domain.
explanation: Cloudflare API tokens look like 40-char hex strings and grant DNS / WAF / Workers access.
fixSteps: Revoke the token in Cloudflare Dashboard → My Profile → API Tokens
Create a new token with the minimum required zone and permission scopes
Restrict the new token by IP allowlist and set an expiry date

codeExamples.label: Cloudflare — create scoped token via API
codeExamples.code:
# Verify a token's permissions before use
curl -X GET 'https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/user/tokens/verify' \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN'

# List all tokens to find the compromised one
curl -X GET 'https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/user/tokens' \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN'

# Revoke it
curl -X DELETE 'https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/user/tokens/TOKEN_ID' \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN'

codeExamples.label: Use environment variable (never hard-code)

title: Tailscale auth key in source
description: tskey-* values allow joining your tailnet. Rotate immediately if exposed.
evidence: Tailscale auth key (tskey-*) detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: An exposed Tailscale auth key allows an attacker to join your private tailnet — giving them full network-level access to every machine in your VPN, including databases, internal services, and admin panels that are not otherwise internet-accessible.
explanation: tskey-* values allow joining your tailnet. Rotate immediately if exposed.
fixSteps: Revoke the key immediately in the Tailscale admin console under Settings → Keys
Check for any unauthorized devices added to your tailnet
Use ephemeral keys with short TTLs for automated systems; store them in a secrets manager

codeExamples.label: Revoke Tailscale key via API
codeExamples.code:
# List auth keys to find the compromised one
curl -H 'Authorization: Bearer TAILSCALE_API_KEY' \
  'https://api.tailscale.com/api/v2/tailnet/YOUR_TAILNET/keys'

# Delete the compromised key (replace KEY_ID)
curl -X DELETE \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer TAILSCALE_API_KEY' \
  'https://api.tailscale.com/api/v2/tailnet/YOUR_TAILNET/keys/KEY_ID'

codeExamples.label: Store auth key as a secret (never in source)
codeExamples.code:
# Store in environment / secrets manager
export TS_AUTHKEY="tskey-auth-..."

# Use in Docker or systemd, not in code:
docker run -e TS_AUTHKEY="$TS_AUTHKEY" tailscale/tailscale

title: Algolia admin API key in source
description: Algolia admin keys (long base64 strings) grant full search-index control.
evidence: Algolia admin API key detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: An exposed Algolia admin key grants full control over all search indices — creating, deleting, and overwriting index data, creating new API keys, and accessing all records in every index. An attacker can corrupt search results site-wide or exfiltrate all indexed content.
explanation: Algolia admin keys (long base64 strings) grant full search-index control.
fixSteps: Never expose the admin key to client code — use the search-only API key in browsers
Rotate the admin key in Algolia Dashboard → Settings → API Keys
Use Algolia's virtual API keys with per-user restrictions for authenticated search

codeExamples.label: Correct: search-only key in client, admin key server-side

title: Mapbox secret token in source
description: sk.* Mapbox tokens grant upload / dataset access. Use pk.* for client.
evidence: Mapbox secret token (sk.*) detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: An exposed Mapbox sk.* (secret) token grants write access to your datasets, styles, and tilesets — an attacker can modify or delete your map data, upload malicious content, and incur charges on your account. Public tokens (pk.*) are fine in browsers; secret tokens must stay server-side.
explanation: sk.* Mapbox tokens grant upload / dataset access. Use pk.* for client.
fixSteps: Revoke the sk.* token in Mapbox Account → Tokens
Use pk.* (public) tokens in client-side code
Restrict the pk.* token by URL to prevent abuse by other sites

codeExamples.label: Correct token usage — public token in browser

title: PagerDuty REST API key in source
description: PD keys grant incident / on-call rotation control.
evidence: PagerDuty API key detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: An exposed PagerDuty API key lets an attacker silence real incidents, reassign on-call rotations, acknowledge alerts automatically, and query your team structure — potentially hiding a real security breach by suppressing the alerts that would trigger a response.
explanation: PD keys grant incident / on-call rotation control.
fixSteps: Rotate the key in PagerDuty → User Settings → API Access Keys
Use app-level API keys with minimum required scopes
Restrict key usage to server-side webhook handlers only

codeExamples.label: Store PagerDuty key as environment variable

title: Twilio Account SID in source
description: Twilio Account SIDs (AC*) are not strictly secret but should not be public.
evidence: Twilio Account SID (AC*) detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Twilio Account SIDs are semi-public and require a paired auth token to perform any sensitive operations. However, knowing your Account SID combined with other leaked credentials enables account takeover. The SID alone leaks your Twilio account association.
explanation: Twilio Account SIDs (AC*) are not strictly secret but should not be public.
fixSteps: Move the SID to a server-side environment variable as a precaution
Ensure the paired Twilio auth token is never exposed client-side
If the auth token is also exposed, rotate it immediately in the Twilio console

codeExamples.label: Store Twilio credentials server-side only

title: Datadog API key in source
description: Datadog API keys grant metric ingestion. Restrict by hostname tag.
evidence: Datadog API key detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: An exposed Datadog API key allows an attacker to flood your dashboards with fake metrics and events, masking real performance issues or triggering false alert storms — and potentially silencing real incident alerts during an attack.
explanation: Datadog API keys grant metric ingestion. Restrict by hostname tag.
fixSteps: Rotate the key in Datadog → Organization Settings → API Keys
Restrict the key by host tag and scope to minimum required permissions
Use the client token (pub_*) for browser RUM, not the API key

title: HuggingFace write token in source
description: hf_* tokens grant model / dataset upload access.
evidence: HuggingFace write token (hf_*) detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: An exposed HuggingFace write token lets an attacker upload malicious model weights or datasets under your account identity — a supply-chain attack that affects anyone who downloads and runs those models, potentially including your own users.
explanation: hf_* tokens grant model / dataset upload access.
fixSteps: Revoke the token at huggingface.co/settings/tokens

codeExamples.label: Use read-only token for inference
codeExamples.code:
from huggingface_hub import InferenceClient
import os

# Use a read-only token for inference — never a write token
client = InferenceClient(
    model="mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.1",
    token=os.environ['HF_READ_TOKEN'],  # read-only token from env
)

title: Pinecone API key in source
description: Pinecone keys grant vector-DB control.
evidence: Pinecone API key detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: An exposed Pinecone API key grants full control over your vector database — an attacker can delete all vectors (destroying your semantic search), upsert poisoned embeddings, query all indexed data, or create new indexes at your expense.
explanation: Pinecone keys grant vector-DB control.
fixSteps: Rotate the key in the Pinecone console under API Keys
Keep Pinecone API calls server-side only; never expose the key in browser bundles
Use environment-scoped keys when available

codeExamples.label: Server-only Pinecone client

title: Supabase service_role JWT in source
description: The service_role JWT bypasses Row Level Security. Should NEVER be in client code.
evidence: Supabase service_role JWT detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: The Supabase service_role key bypasses all Row Level Security (RLS) policies, giving anyone who has it unrestricted read and write access to every row in every table in your database — equivalent to handing over your database root password.
explanation: The service_role JWT bypasses Row Level Security. Should NEVER be in client code.
fixSteps: Immediately remove the service_role key from any client-accessible code
Use the anon key + RLS policies in client code
If exposed, rotate the service_role key in Supabase Dashboard → Settings → API

codeExamples.label: Correct: anon key in client, service_role server-only

title: Supabase anon key in source
description: Supabase anon keys are public by design. Security depends on RLS policies.
evidence: Supabase anon key detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Supabase anon keys are intentionally public — their presence alone is not a vulnerability. The risk is that missing or overly permissive Row Level Security (RLS) policies combined with the key allow unauthenticated users to read or write data they should not access.
explanation: Supabase anon keys are public by design. Security depends on RLS policies.
fixSteps: Enable RLS on every table: ALTER TABLE your_table ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY
Add explicit policies for each role (anon, authenticated)
Test RLS with the Supabase Policy Simulator before deploying

codeExamples.label: Enable RLS and create policies
codeExamples.code:
-- Enable RLS on the table
ALTER TABLE posts ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;

-- Only authenticated users can read
CREATE POLICY "Authenticated users can read posts"
  ON posts FOR SELECT
  TO authenticated
  USING (true);

-- Users can only update their own posts
CREATE POLICY "Users can update own posts"
  ON posts FOR UPDATE
  TO authenticated
  USING (auth.uid() = user_id);

-- No anonymous access
CREATE POLICY "No anon access"
  ON posts FOR ALL
  TO anon
  USING (false);

title: AWS Access Key ID in source
description: AKIA* keys are not secrets by themselves but pair with secret keys.
evidence: AWS Access Key ID (AKIA*) detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: An exposed AWS Access Key ID is a partial credential — it is harmless without the matching secret key. However, its exposure signals that secret keys may also be present in the same codebase, and the Key ID is needed to rotate or audit usage even after compromise.
explanation: AKIA* keys are not secrets by themselves but pair with secret keys.
fixSteps: Search the same codebase for the matching SecretAccessKey (40-char base64 string)
Rotate both keys via IAM if both are present or if exposure is uncertain
Move to IAM Roles and short-lived STS tokens to eliminate long-lived key pairs entirely

codeExamples.label: Detect co-located secret key
codeExamples.code:
# Search for the matching secret key near the Access Key ID
# AWS Secret Access Keys are 40-char base64 strings
grep -rE '[A-Za-z0-9/+=]{40}' --include='*.js' --include='*.ts' --include='*.env' .

# If found, rotate immediately:
aws iam create-access-key --user-name YOUR_USER
aws iam update-access-key --access-key-id AKIA... --status Inactive --user-name YOUR_USER

codeExamples.label: Use IAM Roles instead of key pairs

title: PEM private key in source
description: -----BEGIN ... PRIVATE KEY----- blocks grant signing/decryption access.
evidence: PEM private key block (-----BEGIN ... PRIVATE KEY-----) detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: An exposed PEM private key lets an attacker impersonate your service in TLS handshakes, forge signed JWTs or documents, decrypt intercepted encrypted traffic, or assume any identity associated with the key — depending on how the key is used.
explanation: -----BEGIN ... PRIVATE KEY----- blocks grant signing/decryption access.
fixSteps: Revoke the key immediately (TLS: re-issue certificate; JWT: rotate signing key and invalidate tokens)
Store private keys in a KMS (AWS KMS, HashiCorp Vault, Azure Key Vault) — never in source
Use hardware-backed key storage (HSM or cloud KMS) for production signing operations

codeExamples.label: Load private key from environment variable (not source)
AWS KMS — sign without local private key

title: JWT in client-side config
description: JWTs in source are at minimum replayable; if signed with HS256 and the secret is also leaked, attackers forge tokens.
evidence: JWT token (eyJ*) detected in client-accessible resource or config at {{url}}.
riskImpact: A JWT hard-coded in client config is replayable until it expires. If it has no expiry or was signed with HS256 and the secret is elsewhere in the codebase, an attacker can replay it indefinitely or use the secret to forge tokens for any user or role.
explanation: JWTs in source are at minimum replayable; if signed with HS256 and the secret is also leaked, attackers forge tokens.
fixSteps: Remove the JWT from source; issue tokens at runtime via an auth endpoint
Store session tokens in HttpOnly cookies so JavaScript cannot read them
If the token was used as a service credential, treat it as compromised and rotate

codeExamples.label: Issue tokens at runtime — never hard-code

title: Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI) credentials in source
description: Oracle config files (.oci/config), user OCIDs, tenancy OCIDs, and API signing keys (PEM) grant full tenancy access.
evidence: Oracle Cloud Infrastructure credentials detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Exposed OCI credentials grant full programmatic access to your Oracle Cloud tenancy at the IAM policy level of the compromised user — including compute instances, databases, object storage, and network infrastructure.
explanation: Oracle config files (.oci/config), user OCIDs, tenancy OCIDs, and API signing keys (PEM) grant full tenancy access.
fixSteps: Delete the compromised API key in OCI Console → Identity → Users → API Keys
Create a new key pair and upload only the public key to OCI
Move to Instance Principals or Resource Principals to eliminate static credentials

codeExamples.label: Store OCI config securely (never in source)
codeExamples.code:
# Store the private key in a secrets manager, not in source
# Example with HashiCorp Vault:
vault kv put secret/oci private_key=@~/.oci/oci_api_key.pem

# In CI: set as a masked variable, not a config file
export OCI_CLI_KEY_CONTENT="$(vault kv get -field=private_key secret/oci)"

codeExamples.label: Use Instance Principal (no static credentials needed)
codeExamples.code:
import oci

# Use Instance Principal on OCI compute — no key file needed
signer = oci.auth.signers.InstancePrincipalsSecurityTokenSigner()
object_storage = oci.object_storage.ObjectStorageClient(config={}, signer=signer)

title: IBM Cloud IAM API key in source
description: IBM IAM API keys (32+ char strings) grant access to the entire IBM Cloud account - Kubernetes, databases, Object Storage.
evidence: IBM Cloud IAM API key detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: An exposed IBM Cloud IAM API key grants programmatic access to your IBM Cloud account at the IAM policy level of the key owner — potentially including Kubernetes clusters, databases, object storage, and Watson AI services.
explanation: IBM IAM API keys (32+ char strings) grant access to the entire IBM Cloud account - Kubernetes, databases, Object Storage.
fixSteps: Delete the key immediately: ibmcloud iam api-key-delete KEY_NAME
Create a service ID with minimum required policies and a new API key scoped to it
Store the new key in IBM Secrets Manager, not in environment variables

codeExamples.label: Rotate IBM Cloud IAM key
codeExamples.code:
# Log in and delete the compromised key
ibmcloud login --apikey $COMPROMISED_KEY
ibmcloud iam api-key-delete COMPROMISED_KEY_NAME --force

# Create a new key with a service ID instead of a user key
ibmcloud iam service-id-create my-service --description 'Limited access service'
ibmcloud iam service-policy-create my-service \
  --roles Reader --service-name cloud-object-storage
ibmcloud iam service-api-key-create my-key my-service

title: RubyGems API key in source
description: RubyGems API keys (rubygems_* / older 32-hex) allow publishing gems as the user, enabling supply-chain takeover.
evidence: RubyGems API key detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: An exposed RubyGems API key allows an attacker to publish a new malicious version of any gem you own — injecting backdoors into every Ruby application that runs bundle update or gem update, a direct supply-chain attack with wide blast radius.
explanation: RubyGems API keys (rubygems_* / older 32-hex) allow publishing gems as the user, enabling supply-chain takeover.
fixSteps: Revoke the key at rubygems.org → Edit Profile → API Keys
Enable MFA on your RubyGems account to prevent future unauthorized access
Audit recent gem publishes for unauthorized versions

codeExamples.label: GitHub Actions — use Trusted Publishing (OIDC)
codeExamples.code:
# Use RubyGems Trusted Publishing instead of API keys
jobs:
  publish:
    permissions:
      id-token: write
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: rubygems/release-gem@v1
        # No RUBYGEMS_API_KEY secret needed with Trusted Publishing

title: NuGet API key in source
description: NuGet API keys (oy2_*) allow pushing new package versions to nuget.org under the user's identity - direct supply-chain attack.
evidence: NuGet API key (oy2_*) detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: An exposed NuGet API key lets an attacker push malicious versions of your .NET packages to nuget.org — injecting backdoors into every project that adds or updates those packages, a supply-chain attack affecting .NET developers worldwide.
explanation: NuGet API keys (oy2_*) allow pushing new package versions to nuget.org under the user's identity - direct supply-chain attack.
fixSteps: Revoke the key at nuget.org → Account → API Keys
Audit recent package pushes for unauthorized versions
Use scoped API keys tied to specific packages with push-only permissions

codeExamples.label: GitHub Actions — use Trusted Publishing (OIDC, no key needed)
codeExamples.code:
jobs:
  publish:
    permissions:
      id-token: write
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: dotnet pack -c Release
      - uses: actions/setup-dotnet@v4
      - run: dotnet nuget push '**/*.nupkg' --source https://api.nuget.org/v3/index.json
        env:
          # Only use API key if Trusted Publishing is not available
          NUGET_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.NUGET_API_KEY }}

title: JFrog Artifactory API key in source
description: Artifactory access tokens and encrypted passwords grant upload, delete, and admin over artifact repositories.
evidence: JFrog Artifactory API key or access token detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: An exposed JFrog Artifactory token can be used to upload malicious artifacts, overwrite existing package versions with backdoored builds, delete critical artifacts, or exfiltrate proprietary build outputs — a supply-chain attack on your internal build system.
explanation: Artifactory access tokens and encrypted passwords grant upload, delete, and admin over artifact repositories.
fixSteps: Revoke the token in JFrog Platform → Administration → User Management → Access Tokens
Audit recent artifact uploads for unauthorized changes
Use short-lived access tokens (TTL < 1 hour) for CI/CD pipelines

codeExamples.label: JFrog CLI — rotate access token
codeExamples.code:
# Revoke the compromised token (requires admin token)
curl -X DELETE \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer ADMIN_TOKEN' \
  'https://your-org.jfrog.io/access/api/v1/tokens/COMPROMISED_TOKEN_ID'

# Create a short-lived token for CI (expires in 1 hour)
curl -X POST \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer ADMIN_TOKEN' \
  -d '{"expires_in": 3600, "scope": "applied-permissions/user"}' \
  'https://your-org.jfrog.io/access/api/v1/tokens'

title: New Relic browser key in source
description: New Relic browser license keys (NRBR-*) are shipped in client JS to report RUM data. They are scoped by allowlist but still leak account info.
evidence: New Relic browser license key (NRBR-*) detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: New Relic browser keys are intentionally public for RUM collection but an unrestricted key lets any site submit events billed to your account. Exposed server-side ingest keys (different from browser keys) would allow an attacker to inject fake metrics and suppress real alerts.
explanation: New Relic browser license keys (NRBR-*) are shipped in client JS to report RUM data. They are scoped by allowlist but still leak account info.
fixSteps: Restrict the browser key by hostname allowlist in New Relic Account Settings
Ensure server-side ingest keys (different from browser keys) are never shipped to the browser
Rotate periodically and audit usage for unexpected event sources

codeExamples.label: Restrict browser key by hostname (New Relic UI)
codeExamples.code:
# New Relic browser keys are restricted via the UI, not via API
# Go to: New Relic → Account Settings → Browser Keys → Edit → Add Allowed Hosts
# Allowed hosts: yourdomain.com, *.yourdomain.com

# Verify the key is scoped correctly:
curl 'https://api.newrelic.com/v2/browser_apps.json' \
  -H 'X-Api-Key: YOUR_INGEST_KEY'

title: GitLab deploy token in source
description: GitLab deploy tokens grant push/pull to the project registry and package registry. Long-lived and project-scoped - very useful to attackers.
evidence: GitLab deploy token detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: An exposed GitLab deploy token lets an attacker pull private container images and packages, or push malicious versions — compromising your container supply chain and any system that pulls from your registry.
explanation: GitLab deploy tokens grant push/pull to the project registry and package registry. Long-lived and project-scoped - very useful to attackers.
fixSteps: Revoke the token: GitLab Project → Settings → Repository → Deploy tokens → Revoke

For CI/CD, use group-level access tokens with minimum required scopes

codeExamples.label: Use CI/CD job token instead of deploy token
codeExamples.code:
# .gitlab-ci.yml
docker_pull:
  script:
    # Use the automatically-provided CI_JOB_TOKEN (expires after the job)
    - docker login -u $CI_REGISTRY_USER \
        -p $CI_JOB_TOKEN \
        $CI_REGISTRY
    - docker pull $CI_REGISTRY_IMAGE:latest
    # Never use a hard-coded deploy token here

title: GitLab runner registration token in source
description: Runner registration tokens allow anyone to attach a malicious runner and exfiltrate CI secrets and source from every job.
evidence: GitLab runner registration token detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: An exposed runner registration token allows an attacker to register a malicious runner that intercepts CI/CD jobs — stealing all CI environment variables, source code, deploy keys, and any secrets passed to pipelines. This is a complete CI/CD supply-chain compromise.
explanation: Runner registration tokens allow anyone to attach a malicious runner and exfiltrate CI secrets and source from every job.
fixSteps: Rotate the registration token immediately in GitLab → Admin → Runners or Project → Settings → CI/CD
Unregister all unknown runners and audit recently registered ones
Switch to the new runner authentication token workflow (group-runner auth tokens)

codeExamples.label: Rotate runner registration token via API
codeExamples.code:
# Reset the registration token for a project
curl --request POST \
  --header 'PRIVATE-TOKEN: YOUR_ADMIN_TOKEN' \
  'https://gitlab.example.com/api/v4/projects/PROJECT_ID/runners/reset_registration_token'

# List all registered runners to find unauthorized ones
curl --header 'PRIVATE-TOKEN: YOUR_ADMIN_TOKEN' \
  'https://gitlab.example.com/api/v4/runners/all?status=online'

title: Meilisearch master key in source
description: The Meilisearch master key grants full control: index/document CRUD, tenant creation, and key issuance.
evidence: Meilisearch master key detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: An exposed Meilisearch master key gives an attacker complete control over your search engine — deleting all indexes, corrupting search data, creating API keys for persistent access, and reading all indexed documents including any PII in the search corpus.
explanation: The Meilisearch master key grants full control: index/document CRUD, tenant creation, and key issuance.
fixSteps: Restart Meilisearch with a new master key (set MEILI_MASTER_KEY env var)
Create a scoped search-only API key for browser use: POST /keys with searchRoutes only

codeExamples.label: Create scoped keys — never use master key in app code
codeExamples.code:
# Create a search-only key for browser use (safe to expose)
curl -X POST 'http://localhost:7700/keys' \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer MASTER_KEY' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"actions":["search"],"indexes":["products"],"expiresAt":null}'

# Create an indexing key for server-side use only
curl -X POST 'http://localhost:7700/keys' \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer MASTER_KEY' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"actions":["documents.add","documents.delete"],"indexes":["products"],"expiresAt":null}'

title: Keycloak realm signing key in source
description: Keycloak realm RSA/EC private keys sign every JWT issued by the realm. A leaked key lets attackers mint valid tokens for any user.
evidence: Keycloak realm signing key detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: A leaked Keycloak realm signing key allows an attacker to mint valid JWTs for any user in the realm — including realm-admin — with any roles, claims, or expiry they choose, effectively bypassing all authentication for every application in the realm.
explanation: Keycloak realm RSA/EC private keys sign every JWT issued by the realm. A leaked key lets attackers mint valid tokens for any user.
fixSteps: Rotate via Realm Settings → Keys → Active → click the provider → Regenerate
After rotation, all existing tokens signed with the old key become invalid immediately
Store realm export backups (which contain signing keys) in an encrypted secrets store, not in source

codeExamples.label: Keycloak — force key rotation via Admin CLI
codeExamples.code:
# Use the Keycloak Admin CLI to rotate the realm key
/opt/keycloak/bin/kcadm.sh config credentials \
  --server http://localhost:8080 \
  --realm master \
  --user admin --password admin

# Add a new RSA key provider (higher priority will be used for new tokens)
/opt/keycloak/bin/kcadm.sh create components \
  -r YOUR_REALM \
  -f - <<EOF
{
  "name": "rsa-generated",
  "providerId": "rsa-generated",
  "providerType": "org.keycloak.keys.KeyProvider",
  "config": { "priority": ["200"] }
}
EOF

title: GitHub Personal Access Token in source
description: GitHub classic PATs (ghp_*) grant broad access to repositories and the GitHub API, often with more permissions than intended.
evidence: GitHub Personal Access Token detected in client-accessible resource at {{url}}.
riskImpact: A classic GitHub PAT is often scoped to 'repo' (all repositories) and does not expire by default — a single exposed token can give an attacker persistent read and write access to all private repositories, GitHub Actions secrets, and package registries under the account.
explanation: Classic GitHub Personal Access Tokens have no expiry and are often over-privileged. Fine-grained tokens are scoped to specific repos with mandatory expiry.
fixSteps: Revoke the token immediately at github.com/settings/tokens
Audit the token's recent usage in the GitHub security log
Replace with a fine-grained PAT scoped to specific repositories with an expiry date

codeExamples.label: Fine-grained PAT — minimum scope, mandatory expiry
codeExamples.code:
# Create a fine-grained token at:
# GitHub Settings → Developer Settings → Fine-grained tokens → Generate new token
#
# Choose:
# - Expiration: 30 or 90 days (never 'No expiration')
# - Repository access: Selected repositories only
# - Permissions: minimum required (e.g., Contents: Read-only for CI)

# Store in secrets manager or GitHub Actions secrets — never in source
export GITHUB_TOKEN="github_pat_..."

codeExamples.label: Use GITHUB_TOKEN in Actions (no PAT needed)
codeExamples.code:
jobs:
  build:
    permissions:
      contents: read      # Only the minimum needed
      packages: write     # Only if publishing packages
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}  # Auto-provided, scoped to the run
